code: # Remove zeros to guarantee Gamma requirements airquality_clean <- subset(airquality, Ozone > 0) # Gamma model with log mapping model_gamma <- glm(Ozone ~ Wind, data = airquality_clean, family = Gamma(link = "log"))  model_gamma$aic ## [1] 1040.021 # Extract standardised residuals std_dev_res <- rstandard(model_gamma, type = "deviance") # Validate using the Shapiro-Wilk normality test shapiro.test(std_dev_res) ##  ##  Shapiro-Wilk normality test ##  ## data:  std_dev_res ## W = 0.99245, p-value = 0.7817 # Validate using the Anderson-Darling normality test nortest::ad.test(std_dev_res) ##  ##  Anderson-Darling normality test ##  ## data:  std_dev_res ## A = 0.198, p-value = 0.8853

text: function visstat() accepts input three ways: standardised form, x y must vectors class "numeric", "integer", "factor". formula interface, formula y ~ x specifies response y predictor x variables, data data frame containing variables. backward-compatible form, "namex" "namey" must character strings naming columns data.frame named dataframe. column must class "numeric", "integer", "factor". equivalent writing: simplify notation, throughout remainder, data class numeric integer referred common mode numeric, data class factor referred categorical. interpretation x y depends classes: one numeric factor, numeric must passed response y factor predictor x. supports tests central tendencies. numeric, simple linear regression model fitted y response x predictor. factors (ordered), test association performed (Chi-squared FisherŌĆÖs exact). test symmetric, plot layout depends variable supplied x. factors y additionally class ordered, non parametric test performed: Wilcoxon test case two factor levels x, Kruskal-Wallis-Test two factor levels x. x y class ordered, order levels carries information Chi-squared / Fisher discard. visstat() instead tests monotone association via KendallŌĆÖs Žäb\tau_b (cor.test(..., method = "kendall")) visualises relationship jittered rankŌĆōrank scatter plus mosaic plot.

code: set.seed(123)  # Create predictor: Customer segment (2 groups) segment <- factor(rep(c("Budget", "Premium"), each = 50))  # Create response: Likert scale ratings (1-5) satisfaction_numeric <- c(   sample(1:5, 50, replace = TRUE, prob = c(0.15, 0.25, 0.30, 0.20, 0.10)),  # Budget   sample(1:5, 50, replace = TRUE, prob = c(0.05, 0.10, 0.20, 0.35, 0.30))   # Premium )  # Create dataframe with ORDERED response survey_data <- data.frame(   segment = segment,   satisfaction = ordered(satisfaction_numeric)  # Declare as ordered )  # triggers warnings and use Wilcoxon test result <- visstat(survey_data$segment,survey_data$satisfaction)

text: model fitted linear model object (lm()).

title: Breusch-Pagan Test for Heteroscedasticity ŌĆö bp.test
text: object class "htest" components: statistic value chi-squared test statistic. parameter degrees freedom. p.value p-value test. method character string indicating test performed. data.name character string giving name model.

title: Breusch-Pagan Test for Heteroscedasticity ŌĆö bp.test
text: Breusch-Pagan test regresses squared standardized residuals fitted values. test statistic : $$BP = n \cdot R^2$$ : \(n\) = sample size \(R^2\) = coefficient determination auxiliary regression         \(e_i^2\) \(\hat{y}_i\) null hypothesis homoscedasticity, test statistic follows chi-squared distribution: \(BP \sim \chi^2(p-1)\) \(p\) number parameters model (including intercept). Large values test statistic (small p-values) provide evidence homoscedasticity.

title: Breusch-Pagan Test for Heteroscedasticity ŌĆö bp.test
text: Breusch, T. S., Pagan, . R. (1979). simple test heteroscedasticity random coefficient variation. Econometrica, 47(5), 1287-1294. DOI: 10.2307/1911963

title: Breusch-Pagan Test for Heteroscedasticity ŌĆö bp.test
code: # Example with homoscedastic errors set.seed(123) x <- runif(100) y <- 2 + 3*x + rnorm(100, sd = 1) model1 <- lm(y ~ x) bp.test(model1)  # Should not reject (p > 0.05) #>  #> 	Breusch-Pagan test for heteroscedasticity #>  #> data:  model1 #> BP = 0.025212, df = 1, p-value = 0.8738 #>   # Example with heteroscedastic errors (variance increases with x) set.seed(456) x <- runif(100) y <- 2 + 3 *x + rnorm(100, sd = 0.5 + 2*x) model2 <- lm(y ~ x) bp.test(model2)  # Should reject (p < 0.05) #>  #> 	Breusch-Pagan test for heteroscedasticity #>  #> data:  model2 #> BP = 20.435, df = 1, p-value = 6.169e-06 #>

text: Performs Levene's test using Brown-Forsythe  median-centred modification.  tests null hypothesis groups equal variances testing whether absolute deviations  group  medians equal across groups  function reproduces default behaviour  leveneTest(y,g,center=median,...) car-package.

text: object class "htest" components: statistic value F-statistic. parameter degrees freedom: df1=k-1, df3=N-k, k number groups N total sample size p.value p-value test. method character string indicating test performed. data.name character string giving name(s) data.

title: Brown-Forsythe Levene-type Test for Homogeneity of Variance (center = median) ŌĆö levene.test
text: observation \(y_{ij}\) group \(\), compute absolute deviation group median: $$z_{ij} = |y_{ij} - \tilde{y}_i|$$ \(\tilde{y}_i\) median group \(\). test statistic F-statistic one-way ANOVA \(z_{ij}\) values: $$F = \frac{(N-k) \sum_{=1}^{k} n_i (\bar{z}_i - \bar{z})^2}{(k-1) \sum_{=1}^{k} \sum_{j=1}^{n_i} (z_{ij} - \bar{z}_i)^2}$$ : \(k\) = number groups \(N\) = total sample size \(n_i\) = sample size group \(\) \(\bar{z}_i\) = mean absolute deviations group \(\) \(\bar{z}\) = overall mean absolute deviations null hypothesis equal variances, test statistic follows F-distribution: \(F \sim F(k-1, N-k)\).

title: Brown-Forsythe Levene-type Test for Homogeneity of Variance (center = median) ŌĆö levene.test
text: Brown, M. B., Forsythe, . B. (1974). Robust tests equality variances. Journal American Statistical Association, 69(346), 364ŌĆō367. DOI: 10.1080/01621459.1974.10482955

title: Brown-Forsythe Levene-type Test for Homogeneity of Variance (center = median) ŌĆö levene.test
code: set.seed(123) y <- c(rnorm(10), rnorm(10, sd = 2), rnorm(10, sd = 0.5)) g <- factor(rep(1:3, each = 10)) levene.test(y, g) #>  #> 	Brown-Forsythe Levene-type Test (center = median) #>  #> data:  absolute deviations from group medians (for ANOVA on spread differences) #> F = 3.3913, df1 = 2, df2 = 27, p-value = 0.04853 #>   # Usage with data frame df <- data.frame(response = y, group = g) levene.test(response, group, data = df) #>  #> 	Brown-Forsythe Levene-type Test (center = median) #>  #> data:  absolute deviations from group medians (for ANOVA on spread differences) #> F = 3.3913, df1 = 2, df2 = 27, p-value = 0.04853 #>   # Example with unequal variances (should reject null hypothesis) set.seed(456) y_unequal <- c(rnorm(15, sd = 1), rnorm(15, sd = 5), rnorm(15, sd = 0.2)) g_unequal <- factor(rep(c("A", "B", "C"), each = 15)) levene.test(y_unequal, g_unequal) #>  #> 	Brown-Forsythe Levene-type Test (center = median) #>  #> data:  absolute deviations from group medians (for ANOVA on spread differences) #> F = 31.384, df1 = 2, df2 = 42, p-value = 4.607e-09 #>

title: ANOVA or Welch's ANOVA with appropriate post-hoc tests ŌĆö vis_anova
text: function first tests homogeneity variance using Levene's test. variances equal (p > alpha), Fisher's one-way ANOVA Tukey's HSD post-hoc performed. variances unequal (p <= alpha), Welch's one-way ANOVA Games-Howell post-hoc performed. function produces box plot jittered points group means (red diamonds parametric branches), annotated compact letter display showing groups differ significantly.

code: # Example with equal variances (uses Fisher's ANOVA + TukeyHSD) data(PlantGrowth) result1 <- vis_anova(PlantGrowth$weight, PlantGrowth$group,                       samplename = "Weight", factorname = "Group")   # Example with unequal variances (uses Welch's ANOVA + Games-Howell) # Create data with heterogeneous variances set.seed(123) group_a <- rnorm(20, mean = 10, sd = 1) group_b <- rnorm(20, mean = 15, sd = 5)  # Much larger variance group_c <- rnorm(20, mean = 12, sd = 2) values <- c(group_a, group_b, group_c) groups <- factor(rep(c("A", "B", "C"), each = 20)) result2 <- vis_anova(values, groups,                       samplename = "Value", factorname = "Group")

text: wrapper supports three input formats: (1) Formula interface: visstat(y ~ x, data = df), formula specifies response (y) predictor (x) variables, data data frame containing variables. (2) Standardised form: visstat(x, y), x y vectors class "numeric", "integer", "factor". x predictor grouping variable y response variable. (3) Backward-compatible form: visstat(dataframe, "name_of_y", "name_of_x"), first character string refers response variable second predictor grouping variable. must column names dataframe. interpretation x y depends variable classes. Throughout, data class numeric integer referred numeric, data class factor referred categorical: one variable numeric factor, numeric vector response (y) factor grouping variable (x). supports tests central tendencies (e.g., t-test, Welch's ANOVA, Wilcoxon, Kruskal-Wallis). variables numeric, linear model fitted y response x predictor. variables factors, association test (Chi-squared Fisher's exact) used. test result invariant variable order, visualisations (e.g., axis layout, bar orientation) depend roles x y. wrapper standardises input calls visstat_core, selects executes appropriate test visual output assumption diagnostics.

title: Wrapper for visstat_core Allowing Three Different Input Styles ŌĆö visstat
code: # Formula interface mtcars$am <- as.factor(mtcars$am) visstat(mpg ~ am, data = mtcars)    # Standardised usage visstat(mtcars$am, mtcars$mpg)    # Backward-compatible usage (same result as above) visstat(mtcars, "mpg", "am")    ## Student's t-test (equal variances, two groups) # When residuals are normally distributed and Levene's test indicates # homoscedasticity, the classic Student's t-test with pooled variance is used df <- droplevels(subset(PlantGrowth, group %in% c("ctrl", "trt1"))) visstat(df$group,df$weight)    ## Welch's t-test (unequal variances, two groups) # When residuals are normally distributed but Levene's test indicates # heteroscedasticity, Welch's t-test is used visstat(mtcars$am, mtcars$mpg)    ## Wilcoxon rank sum test (non-normal, two groups) # When residuals are not normally distributed grades_gender <- data.frame(   Sex = as.factor(c(rep("Girl", 20), rep("Boy", 20))),   Grade = c(     19.3, 18.1, 15.2, 18.3, 7.9, 6.2, 19.4, 20.3, 9.3, 11.3,     18.2, 17.5, 10.2, 20.1, 13.3, 17.2, 15.1, 16.2, 17.3, 16.5,     5.1, 15.3, 17.1, 14.8, 15.4, 14.4, 7.5, 15.5, 6.0, 17.4,     7.3, 14.3, 13.5, 8.0, 19.5, 13.4, 17.9, 17.7, 16.4, 15.6   ) ) visstat(grades_gender$Sex, grades_gender$Grade)    ## Fisher's ANOVA (equal variances, >2 groups) # When residuals are normally distributed and Levene's test indicates # homoscedasticity, classic Fisher's ANOVA with TukeyHSD post-hoc is used. # Different green letters indicate significant differences between groups.  visstat(PlantGrowth$group, PlantGrowth$weight)    ## Welch's one-way ANOVA (unequal variances, >2 groups) set.seed(123) values <- c(rnorm(20, 10, 1),rnorm(20, 15, 5),rnorm(20, 12, 2)) groups <- factor(rep(c("A", "B", "C"), each = 20)) visstat(groups, values)   ## Kruskal-Wallis (non-normal, >2 groups) # When residuals are not normally distributed, kruskal.test() is followed by  # pairwise.wilcox.test.  visstat(iris$Species, iris$Petal.Width)    ## Simple linear regression (both numeric) visstat(trees$Height, trees$Girth, conf.level = 0.99)    ## Pearson's Chi-squared test (both factors, large expected counts) HairEyeColorDataFrame <- counts_to_cases(as.data.frame(HairEyeColor)) visstat(HairEyeColorDataFrame$Eye, HairEyeColorDataFrame$Hair)    ## Fisher's exact test (both factors, small expected counts) HairEyeColorMaleFisher <- HairEyeColor[, , 1] blackBrownHazelGreen <- HairEyeColorMaleFisher[1:2, 3:4] blackBrownHazelGreen <- counts_to_cases(as.data.frame(blackBrownHazelGreen)) visstat(blackBrownHazelGreen$Eye, blackBrownHazelGreen$Hair)   ## Save PNG visstat(blackBrownHazelGreen$Hair, blackBrownHazelGreen$Eye,         graphicsoutput = "png", plotDirectory = tempdir())  ## Custom plot name visstat(iris$Species, iris$Petal.Width,         graphicsoutput = "pdf", plotName = "kruskal_iris", plotDirectory = tempdir()) #> Warning: calling par(new=TRUE) with no plot

Throughout, data class numeric integer referred numeric, data class factor referred categorical. significance level alpha defined one minus confidence level, given argument conf.level. For a non-causal alternative #> consider rerunning with correlation = TRUE.  ## Wilcoxon rank sum test (wilcox.test()) grades_gender <- data.frame(   Sex = as.factor(c(rep("Girl", 20), rep("Boy", 20))),   Grade = c(     19.3, 18.1, 15.2, 18.3, 7.9, 6.2, 19.4,     20.3, 9.3, 11.3, 18.2, 17.5, 10.2, 20.1, 13.3, 17.2, 15.1, 16.2, 17.3,     16.5, 5.1, 15.3, 17.1, 14.8, 15.4, 14.4, 7.5, 15.5, 6.0, 17.4,     7.3, 14.3, 13.5, 8.0, 19.5, 13.4, 17.9, 17.7, 16.4, 15.6   ) ) visstat_core(grades_gender, "Grade", "Sex")    ## Welch's oneway ANOVA not assuming equal variances (oneway.test()) anova_npk <- visstat_core(npk, "yield", "block")   anova_npk # prints summary of tests #> Object of class 'visstat' #>  #> Available components: #> [1] "summary statistics of ANOVA" "post-hoc analysis "          #> [3] "conf.level"                  "effect_size"                  ## Kruskal-Wallis rank sum test (kruskal.test()) visstat_core(iris, "Petal.Width", "Species")   visstat_core(InsectSprays, "count", "spray")    ## Simple linear regression  (lm()) visstat_core(trees, "Girth", "Height", conf.level = 0.99)    ## Pearson's Chi-squared test (chisq.test()) ### Transform array to data.frame HairEyeColorDataFrame <- counts_to_cases(as.data.frame(HairEyeColor)) visstat_core(HairEyeColorDataFrame, "Hair", "Eye")    ## Fisher's exact test (fisher.test()) HairEyeColorMaleFisher <- HairEyeColor[, , 1] ### slicing out a 2 x2 contingency table blackBrownHazelGreen <- HairEyeColorMaleFisher[1:2, 3:4] blackBrownHazelGreen <- counts_to_cases(as.data.frame(blackBrownHazelGreen)) fisher_stats <- visstat_core(blackBrownHazelGreen, "Hair", "Eye")

title: visStatistics 0.2.0
text: Unified normality testing across branches. two-group case (t-test vs Wilcoxon) multi-group case (ANOVA vs Kruskal-Wallis) now share normality test: shapiro.test() applied internally studentized residuals lm(y ~ x) via rstandard(). Variance homogeneity test changed bartlett.test() levene.test(). Brown-Forsythe Levene-type test (using median) robust non-normality BartlettŌĆÖs test. test now determines whether StudentŌĆÖs t-test (t.test(var.equal = TRUE)) WelchŌĆÖs t-test used two-group case, whether aov() oneway.test() used multi-group case. Large-sample threshold changed 30 50 per group. every group exceeds 50 observations, normality testing skipped parametric tests applied directly (justified central limit theorem). Post-hoc test selection. oneway.test() (WelchŌĆÖs ANOVA) used unequal variances, games.howell() appropriate post-hoc test, assume equal variances. TukeyHSD() remains aov() (StudentŌĆÖs ANOVA). Rank correlation (correlation = TRUE). Selects appropriate rank correlation data type: SpearmanŌĆÖs Žü\rho two numeric variables, KendallŌĆÖs Žäb\tau_b variables ordered factors. test decision made automatically; requires explicit user choice.

title: visStatistics 0.2.0
text: Formula interface: visstat(y ~ x, data = df) now supported alongside existing visstat(x, y) visstat(dataframe, "namey", "namex") forms. Ordered factor responses: response class ordered, visstat() converts numeric ranks applies Wilcoxon Kruskal-Wallis. variables ordered correlation = TRUE, KendallŌĆÖs Žäb\tau_b used instead. Correlation analysis: New parameter correlation visstat(). set TRUE, selects Spearman rank correlation numeric variables KendallŌĆÖs Žäb\tau_b two ordered factors, instead fitting regression model. New exported functions: levene.test(): Brown-Forsythe Levene-type test homogeneity variance (center = median), mimicking default behaviour leveneTest() car package. bp.test(): Breusch-Pagan test heteroscedasticity linear regression models. games.howell(): Games-Howell post-hoc test pairwise comparisons following WelchŌĆÖs ANOVA. vis_lm_assumptions(): Renamed vis_anova_assumptions(), now provides unified assumption diagnostics general linear model (t-test, ANOVA, regression).
